coredata.dc:description: Wound healing is often impeded by bacterial infections, excessive reactive oxygen species (ROS), and inflammatory responses, challenges that traditional dressings inadequately address. Rutin, a natural flavonoid recognized for its antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects, is limited in biomedical applications because of its low solubility in water. This research presents a novel approach using nitrogen and zinc-doped rutin-derived carbon dots (ZR-CDs), which enhances both antioxidant activity and bioavailability by leveraging the nanoscale properties and surface modifiability of carbon dots. The ZR‑CDs are covalently integrated into an oxidized hyaluronic acid (OHA)/Pluronic F‑127 hydrogel through Schiff‑base chemistry, yielding an injectable, shape‑adaptive composite hydrogel (ZCD@OF), aimed at promoting the healing of infected wounds. In vitro, ZR-CDs exhibit excellent biocompatibility, free-radical scavenging, and protection against ROS-induced cellular dysfunction. The ZCD@OF hydrogel exhibited robust antibacterial efficacy while maintaining favorable rheological properties for wound coverage. In vivo on an infected wound model, ZCD@OF significantly accelerates wound closure, promotes angiogenesis and collagen deposition, and suppresses pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-6, TNF-α). This study highlights the therapeutic potential of ZCD@OF hydrogel in complex wound treatment, illustrating its capacity to synergistically integrate carbon dot with advanced biomaterial strategies for improved therapeutic outcomes.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization and Antioxidant Properties of ZR-CDs. (A) TEM image and histogram of size distribution of ZR-CDs. (B) Elemental mapping of ZR-CDs. (C) Hydrodynamic diameter distribution of ZR-CDs. (D) UV–vis absorption, excitation and emission PL spectra of ZR-CDs. (E) FT-IR spectra of ZR-CDs, NR-CDs and R-CDs. (F) XRD pattern of ZR-CDs and NR-CDs. (G) XPS survey scan spectrum of ZR-CDs, and high-resolution spectra of (H) C 1s, (I) N 1s, (J) O 1s, and (K) Zn 2p of ZR-CDs. (L)Zeta potential of ZR-CDs and R-CDs. (M) ABTS•+ radical scavenging rate, (N) DPPH radical scavenging rate and (O) O2 •− scavenging rate of ZR-CDs and R-CDs. Data are presented as mean ± SD, n = 3, *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001.

Characterization of ZCD@OF hydrogel. (A) Digital images of sol-gel transition showing the ZCD@OF hydrogel at 4 °C and 37 °C. (B) SEM images of pure Pluronic F-127 and ZCD@OF hydrogel. Scale bar: 100 µm. (C) FT-IR spectra of OHA, Pluronic F-127 and ZCD@OF hydrogel. (D) Storage modulus (G′) and loss modulus (G″) of ZCD@OF hydrogel with temperature change. (E) Viscosity against shear rate of ZCD@OF hydrogel. (Inserted image: The injectability of ZCD@OF hydrogel through the syringe.) (F) Swelling ratio of ZCD@OF hydrogel. (G) Remaining weight ratio during degradation of ZCD@OF hydrogel. (H) In vitro drug release behavior of ZCD@OF hydrogel. Data are presented as mean ± SD, n = 3.

In vitro Antibacterial Properties of ZCD@OF hydrogel. (A) Representative images of agar plates showing S. aureus in different groups. (B) Quantitative analysis of the antibacterial rate of S. aureus using colony counting. (C) CLSM images of live/dead bacterial staining. (D) Quantitative analysis of bacterial mortality rate via live/dead bacterial staining assay. (E) Representative crystal violet-stained biofilm images. (F) Quantitative analysis of biofilm inhibition rate. Scale bar: 50 µm. Data are presented as mean ± SD, n = 3, *p < 0.05, ****p < 0.0001. ns: not significant.

Biocompatibility of ZR-CDs. Cell viability of (A) L929 cells, (B) HUVECs, and (C) RAW264.7 cells incubated with different concentrations of ZR-CDs (µg/mL) for 24 h. (D) Blood compatibility of ZR-CDs evaluated by hemolysis assay. (E) Fluorescence images of Calcein-AM/PI staining of HUVECs after incubation with different concentrations of ZR-CDs (µg/mL). (F) Quantitative analysis of the proportion of living and dead cells. Scale bar: 100 µm. Data are presented as mean ± SD, n = 3.

Evaluation of ZR-CDs in Cellular ROS Scavenging and Their Impact on Migration and Angiogenesis under Oxidative Stress. (A, B) Fluorescence microscopy images and quantitative analysis of DCFH-DA stained L929 cells treated with H₂O₂ and varying concentrations of ZR-CDs. Scale bar: 100 µm. (C, D) Fluorescence microscopy images and quantitative analysis of DCFH-DA stained HUVECs. Scale bar: 100 µm. Representative images and quantitative results of (E, F) scratch wound healing and (G, H) Transwell migration assays for HUVECs. Scar bar: 200 µm. (I) Brightfield images of HUVECs tube formation assay. Scar bar: 200 µm. Quantitative analysis of (J) total vessel length and (K) number of nodes. Data are presented as mean ± SD, n = 4, *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001, ****p < 0.0001.

In vivo Assessment of Wound Healing and Antibacterial Efficacy of Infected Full-thickness Skin Defects in Rats. (A) Illustration of the timeline for wound creation and subsequent interventions. (B) Digital images showing wounds with various interventions at different time points. (C)Progression of wound healing tracked from day 0 to day 14. (D) Quantitative analysis of wound closure rate. (E) Representative images of agar plates in different groups. (F) Quantitative analysis of the antibacterial rate using colony counting. Data are presented as mean ± SD, n ≥ 3. *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001, ****p < 0.0001.

Histological evaluation of wound healing. (A, B) Optical images and magnified images of H&E and Masson staining on Day 14. Scale bar: 1 mm for the normal-sized image; 100 µm for the magnified image. (C) Micrographs of IHC staining of CD31, IL-6, and TNF-α on day 14. Scale bar: 100 µm. (D) Statistical analysis of collagen area ratio. (E) Statistical evaluation of CD31-positive blood vessel. (F, G) Statistical analyses of TNF-α and IL-6 expression levels. Data are presented as mean ± SD, n ≥ 3. *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001, ****p < 0.0001, ns: not significant.

Illustration of the synthesis procedure for nitrogen and zinc-doped rutin-derived carbon dots (ZR-CDs) and the ZCD@OF hydrogel, demonstrating their applications and functional characteristics in the regeneration of infected skin defects.

head.ce:title: Novel metal-doped rutin-derived carbon dots in injectable hydrogel for enhanced ROS scavenging and antibacterial effects in wound healing

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Wound healing is often impeded by bacterial infections, excessive reactive oxygen species (ROS), and inflammatory responses, challenges that traditional dressings inadequately address. Rutin, a natural flavonoid recognized for its antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects, is limited in biomedical applications because of its low solubility in water. This research presents a novel approach using nitrogen and zinc-doped rutin-derived carbon dots (ZR-CDs), which enhances both antioxidant activity and bioavailability by leveraging the nanoscale properties and surface modifiability of carbon dots. The ZR‑CDs are covalently integrated into an oxidized hyaluronic acid (OHA)/Pluronic F‑127 hydrogel through Schiff‑base chemistry, yielding an injectable, shape‑adaptive composite hydrogel (ZCD@OF), aimed at promoting the healing of infected wounds. In vitro, ZR-CDs exhibit excellent biocompatibility, free-radical scavenging, and protection against ROS-induced cellular dysfunction. The ZCD@OF hydrogel exhibited robust antibacterial efficacy while maintaining favorable rheological properties for wound coverage. In vivo on an infected wound model, ZCD@OF significantly accelerates wound closure, promotes angiogenesis and collagen deposition, and suppresses pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-6, TNF-α). This study highlights the therapeutic potential of ZCD@OF hydrogel in complex wound treatment, illustrating its capacity to synergistically integrate carbon dot with advanced biomaterial strategies for improved therapeutic outcomes.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The skin acts as the body's primary defense against multiple external risks, such as physical injuries and microbial infiltration. A breach in the skin's structural integrity can precipitate bacterial infections, with an overproduction of reactive oxygen species (ROS) and subsequent inflammation further hindering the healing process, resulting in the development of chronic wounds [1–4]. Thus, it is imperative to develop innovative therapeutic strategies that can effectively manage ROS levels and foster an optimal healing environment [5–7].

Using exogenous antioxidants is a promising strategy for controlling levels of ROS. Although natural enzymes are renowned for their potent ROS scavenging capabilities, their application is hampered by several factors including high production costs, stringent reaction conditions, and limited stability, emphasizing the need for alternatives [8]. Natural compounds, abundant in active antioxidant groups, offer a viable solution due to their inherent ROS scavenging properties [9–12]. Particularly, rutin, a flavonoid present in many plants, is notable for its antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects [13,14]. However, the clinical efficacy of rutin is constrained by its inadequate water solubility and low bioavailability [15].

Carbon dots (CDs), burgeoning as novel nanomaterials, offer a promising solution to these challenges [16]. Characterized by their unique optoelectronic properties, superior biocompatibility, and environmentally friendly synthesis techniques, CDs are increasingly recognized as ideal candidates for applications in drug delivery, bioimaging, and therapeutics [17–19]. Specifically, rutin-derived CDs may preserve key antioxidant moieties of rutin[20], and the nanoscale size and modifiable surface of CDs could significantly improve bioavailability of rutin-derived CDs and expand their potential applications [21]. Metal doping is an effective strategy to enhance the performance of CDs [22,23]. Zinc, an essential trace element in the human body, playing a crucial role in different stages of wound healing not only enhances antioxidant defenses [24] but also supports immune regulation [25,26], cell proliferation [27], angiogenesis [28] and extracellular matrix remodeling [29]. The introduction of zinc not only enriches the chemical composition and electronic structure of CDs [30,31] but also expands the CDs' biological applications such as nanozymes [32], antibacterial activities [33,34] and bioactive molecules delivery platforms [35].

Wound dressings are pivotal in facilitating wound healing [36,37]. However, traditional dressings like gauze and band-aids, fall short in managing complex wounds due to their lack of moisture retention, antibacterial properties, and flexibility [38]. Hydrogels, with their three-dimensional porous structure and excellent biophysical characteristics, have emerged as a focal point of research in wound dressing development [37,39–41]. Composite materials that combine synthetic polymers with natural polysaccharides can maintain a moist wound environment and enhance healing by incorporating nanomaterials [42,43]. Pluronic F127, a triblock copolymer, is noted for its temperature sensitivity and biocompatibility, adapting to wound contours and providing stable protection at body temperature [44]. When enhanced with oxidized hyaluronic acid, the resultant composite hydrogel displays augmented mechanical strength, elasticity, and temperature responsiveness, thereby optimizing the wound healing milieu [45].

In this research, we introduce an innovative injectable and temperature-sensitive hydrogel (ZCD@OF), which incorporates nitrogen and zinc-doped rutin-derived carbon dots (ZR-CDs) within a matrix of oxidized hyaluronic acid (OHA) and Pluronic F-127. ZR-CDs are incorporated into this hydrogel through a Schiff base reaction between the amino groups on their surface and the aldehyde groups in OHA, facilitating the loading of ZR-CDs. Our in vitro analyses reveal that ZR-CDs can effectively modulate intracellular ROS levels, protect cells from functional impairments due to oxidative stress. In vivo assessments on an infected wound model reveal that the ZCD@OF hydrogel exhibits remarkable antibacterial activity, promotes angiogenesis and collagen synthesis, and reduces pro-inflammatory cytokine levels (IL-6 and TNF-α) ( Scheme 1). This research proposes a promising approach for managing complex wounds, showcases the integration of advanced material strategies and carbon dot technology in developing effective solutions for biomedical applications.

Rutin, ethylenediamine, zinc acetate dihydrate, Pluronic F-127, sodium periodate and hyaluronic acid (MW 20–40 kDa), all purchased from Aladdin Industrial Corporation (Shanghai, China). CCK-8 assay kits, Calcein-AM/PI live/dead staining kits, ABTS radical scavenging assay kits, DPPH Free Radical Scavenging Capacity Assay Kit, DCFH-DA, and 0.1 % crystal violet staining solution were obtained from Solarbio (Beijing, China).

L929 cells, human umbilical vein endothelial cells (HUVECs), and RAW264.7 cells were sourced from the Cell Resource Center, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Shanghai, China. The DMEM, FBS, and penicillin-streptomycin solution were obtained from Gibco (USA). Antibodies used included Goat Anti-Rabbit IgG H&L (HRP) (ab6721, Abcam), anti-CD31 (ab182981, Abcam), anti-TNF-α (GB115701, Servicebio), anti-IL-6 (GB11117, Servicebio).

ZR-CDs were synthesized using a modified hydrothermal method. In brief, 0.5 g of rutin, 0.25 g of zinc acetate dihydrate, and 200 µL of ethylenediamine were dissolved in 20 mL of ultrapure water and thoroughly mixed. The resulting solution was then transferred into a 50 mL hydrothermal reactor. To systematically optimize the synthesis parameters, single-factor experiments were conducted with the following variables: reaction temperature (180 °C, 200 °C, or 220 °C) and zinc precursor-to-rutin mass ratio (0.2:1, 0.5:1, or 1:1). The reaction duration was fixed at 5 h for all trials. After the reaction, the system was allowed to cool to room temperature, and the resulting product was subjected to centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 20 min. The supernatant was filtered through a 0.22 µm membrane filter. To further purify the solution, the filtrate was dialyzed using a 500 Da molecular weight cutoff bag for 48 h to remove smaller molecular impurities. The dialyzed solution was then freeze-dried for 24 h to obtain dry ZR-CDs powder, which was stored for subsequent use in further experiments.

Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) analyses were performed with FEI Tecnai G2 F30. Ultraviolet-Visible (UV-Vis) absorption spectra were obtained with a Mapada UV-3000. Photoluminescence (PL) properties were assessed using an FLS980. Fourier Transform Infrared (FT-IR) spectra were captured utilizing a Thermo Fisher Nicolet 6700. Measurements for X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) were executed with a Thermo Scientific EscaLab 250Xi. X-ray Diffraction (XRD) measurements were conducted with a Bruker D8. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) and zeta potential was determined using Brookhaven Omni.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of ZCD@OF Hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: To prepare oxidized hyaluronic acid (OHA), 1.0 g of hyaluronic acid was dissolved in 100 mL of ultrapure water. Then, a 0.1 M sodium periodate solution was gradually added at a ratio of 0.1 g per gram of hyaluronic acid, and the mixture was stirred continuously for 24 h in the dark. To halt the oxidation, ethanol was added after the reaction. The mixture was dialyzed using a bag with a 3.5 kDa molecular weight cutoff to remove residual sodium periodate, changing the dialysis water every 8 h for 72 h. The resulting OHA was lyophilized to yield a white powder, which was stored in a dry environment for later use. To prepare the ZCD@OF hydrogel, 4 % (w/v) OHA and 20 % (w/v) Pluronic F-127 were mixed in cold water and stirred for 4 h, then left at 4 °C overnight. Subsequently, the 10 % (w/v) ZR-CDs were added to the gel and stirred at 4 °C to enhance the interaction between ZR-CDs and OHA/F127. The resulting mixture was stored at 4 °C. The hydrogel's morphology was examined using a scanning electron microscope (SEM, Zeiss sigma300). FT-IR spectra were assessed using a Thermo Fisher Nicolet 6700. Dynamic rheological properties were measured using a HAAKE MARS III rheometer. Temperature-dependent behaviors were assessed via temperature sweep tests ranging from 4 °C to 50 °C with a heating rate of 1 °C/min. Shear thinning test was performed across a shear rate spectrum from 0 to 160 1/s, maintaining the frequency constant at 1 Hz and the strain at 1.0 %.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro evaluation of swelling, degradation and drug release properties of ZCD@OF hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: The lyophilized hydrogel samples were immersed in PBS at 37 °C and incubated for predetermined time intervals to evaluate their swelling behavior. At each time point, the samples were carefully retrieved, and excess surface buffer was removed using filter paper to determine the wet weight (Ms). The swelling ratio (SR) was calculated according to the formula: SR = [(Ms - Mi) / Mi] × 100 %

To evaluate the degradation behavior of the ZCD@OF hydrogel, samples were prepared with dimensions of 15 mm in diameter and 1.5 mm in height. These samples were immersed in 1 mL of PBS solution containing 2 U/mL hyaluronidase at 37 °C. The mass of the hydrogel was measured at specific time intervals to assess degradation. The percentage of residual mass was determined using the equation: Remaining Mass (%) = [(M0 - Mt)/M0] × 100 %, where M0 is the initial mass and Mt is the mass at time t.

The release profile of ZR-CDs from the ZCD@OF hydrogel was studied using the dialysis bag diffusion method. A 2 mL sample of the hydrogel was sealed in a dialysis bag (molecular weight cutoff: 3500 Da) and submerged in 20 mL of PBS (0.1 M, pH 7.4) at 37 °C with continuous stirring at 50 rpm. At specified intervals, 2 mL of the surrounding medium was extracted for analysis and replenished with fresh PBS to maintain consistent conditions. The concentration of the released ZR-CDs was quantified using a UV–visible spectrophotometer at 330 nm.

To assess the scavenging activity against ABTS cationic radicals, a mixture was prepared using 7.4 mM ABTS diammonium salt and 2.6 mM potassium persulfate, and it was allowed to incubate in darkness for 12 h to facilitate the formation of ABTS•+. Subsequently, the solution was diluted with PBS to achieve the desired concentration. Various concentrations of either ZR-CDs or R-CDs were then added to this solution. The mixtures were incubated in the dark for 6 min at room temperature. The scavenging efficiency was quantified by measuring the absorbance at 734 nm.

To determine the scavenging activity of ZR-CDs and R-CDs against DPPH•, a 200 µM solution of DPPH• in ethanol was prepared and treated with various concentrations of either ZR-CDs or R-CDs. The reaction proceeded for 1 h at 25°C, shielded from light. The decrease in DPPH• concentration was monitored by measuring the absorbance at 526 nm.

The scavenging activity of superoxide anions by ZR-CDs and R-CDs was evaluated using nitroblue tetrazolium (NBT). Varying concentrations of either ZR-CDs or R-CDs were mixed with 13 mM L-methionine, 0.05 mM NBT, and 20 µM riboflavin in PBS and incubated at 25 °C for 30 min. The scavenging efficiency was determined by measuring the decrease in absorbance at 560 nm.

Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) ATCC 25923 was initially cultured on solid Luria-Bertani (LB) agar medium. It was then transferred to 5 mL of LB broth and subjected to shaking incubation at 37 °C and 200 rpm for 8 h to reach the logarithmic growth phase. The cultured bacteria were then diluted with PBS to 1 × 106 CFU/mL. The prepared bacterial suspension was then incubated with PBS, hydrogel, ZR-CDs, and the composite ZCD@OF at 37 °C for 8 h. After incubation, serial dilutions were conducted and 100 µL of each dilution was evenly spread onto LB agar plates which were then incubated at 37 °C overnight for CFU enumeration. The antibacterial rate was evaluated by the following formula: Antibacterial rate (%) = (CFU of control − CFU of experimental groups)/CFU of control × 100 %.

Under identical conditions as in vitro antibacterial experiments, samples were incubated at ambient temperature for 8 h. Subsequently, the samples were stained with SYTO-9 and PI dyes for 30 min. Fluorescence images were acquired using a confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM, Olympus FV3000). The bacterial mortality rate (%) was calculated by the following formula: Bacterial mortality rate (%) = (number of dead bacteria/total number of bacteria) × 100 %.

A standardized static biofilm assay was performed by 900 µL of an S. aureus suspension (1 × 10⁷ CFU/mL) was dispensed into each well of a sterile 24-well polystyrene microtiter plate. Subsequently, 100 µL of PBS, hydrogel, ZR-CDs, and the composite ZCD@OF extraction solution was added to each well. The plates were sealed and incubated statically at 37 °C for 48 h. After incubation, non-adherent cells were removed by washing with cold PBS (pH 7.4) three times, and the attached biofilms were fixed with absolute methanol for 15 min then air-dried at room temperature. For staining, 300 µL of 0.1 % crystal violet solution was added to each well, and the plates were incubated in the dark for 20 min. Excess stains were removed, and bound dye was then solubilized by adding 95 % ethanol to each well. The absorbance of the solubilized stain was measured at 595 nm using a microplate reader to quantify biofilm biomass.

The impact of ZR-CDs and ZCD@OF hydrogel on cell viability was evaluated using the Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK-8). L929 cells, HUVECs, and RAW264.7 cells were plated into 96-well plates at a density of 1 × 104 cells per well and cultured for 24 h at 37 °C with 5 % CO2. Subsequently, the medium was replaced with 100 µL of fresh medium containing varying concentrations of ZR-CDs or ZCD@OF hydrogel extract. After 24 h of incubation, 10 % (v/v) CCK-8 reagent was introduced and incubated for 2 h at 37 °C. The optical density at 450 nm was measured using a microplate reader (iMark, Bio-Rad).

The cytotoxicity of ZR-CDs and ZCD@OF hydrogel was evaluated using live/dead cell staining assays. L929 cells and HUVECs were cultured in 96-well plates at a density of 2 × 104 cells per well. Subsequently, the cells were exposed to different concentrations of ZR-CDs or ZCD@OF hydrogel extract for 24 h. Following this exposure, cells were stained with Calcein-AM for 20 min and propidium iodide (PI) for 10 min. Fluorescence images were obtained with the use of an inverted fluorescence microscope (Leica DMi8).

Hemocompatibility was evaluated through hemolysis testing. Fresh rat blood was used to obtain red blood cells, which were then centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 15 min at 4 °C. Subsequently, the cells were rinsed three times with saline solution. A mixture of 3 mL of red blood cells and 11 mL of saline was prepared. Different concentrations of ZR-CDs and hydrogel extract were incubated with a 4 % solution of red blood cells at 37 °C for 2 h. The absorbance was measured at a wavelength of 570 nm using a Bio-Rad iMark microplate reader to calculate the hemolysis rate.

To assess the capacity of ZR-CDs to scavenge intracellular ROS, L929 cells and HUVECs were plated into 96-well plates at a density of 1 × 104 cells per well and subjected to 500 µM H2O2 for 4 h to induce oxidative stress. Subsequently, the cells were treated with various concentrations of ZR-CDs for 24 h, followed by incubation with the DCFH-DA probe in darkness for 30 min. Afterward, the cells were rinsed with PBS. To quantify the levels of ROS, fluorescence intensity was measured using an inverted fluorescence microscope (Leica DMi8).

To assess the effects of ZR-CDs on cellular migration, a scratch assay was performed. L929 cells and HUVECs were seeded in 6-well plates and cultured until confluence. Prior to the experiment, the cells were treated with DMEM containing 500 µM H2O2 to simulate oxidative stress. A sterile 100 µL pipette tip was utilized to create a straight scratch. Subsequently, serum-free medium with different concentrations of ZR-CDs was then added to each well. Images of the scratch were taken at 0 and 24 h and analyzed using ImageJ software.

The impact of ZR-CDs on the migratory behavior of L929 cells and HUVECs was evaluated using the Transwell migration assay. Cells subjected to oxidative stress induction were seeded into the upper chambers of 24-well Transwell plates (Corning, NY, USA) with an 8 µm pore size. The lower chambers contained culture medium with different concentrations of ZR-CDs. After 24 h, the cells were fixed with 4 % paraformaldehyde for 30 min and stained with 0.1 % crystal violet. Excess stain was washed off with PBS. The migrated cells were then photographed using an optical microscope.

The tube formation assay was conducted to evaluate the angiogenic capability of HUVECs under oxidative stress conditions. HUVECs were pre-incubated with 500 µM H2O2 for 4 h to induce oxidative stress, and then treated with varying concentrations of ZR-CDs for 24 h. The treated cells were placed on solidified Matrigel in 96-well plate and were incubated at 37 °C with 5 % CO2 for 6 h. Tubular structures were imaged using an optical microscope. The number of nodes and the total tube length were quantified using ImageJ software.

All animal experiments were approved by the Animal Ethics Committee of Shanxi Medical University (KQDW-2024-003). Male Sprague-Dawley rats, 6–8 weeks and weighing 250–300 g, were utilized in the experiments. Anesthesia was administered via a 50 mg/kg dose of pentobarbital sodium injected intraperitoneally. Two full-thickness 2 cm diameter skin wounds were created on the back of each rat under sterile conditions. A 100 µL suspension containing 1 × 108 CFU/mL S. aureus was applied to the wound area. To assess different treatments, the rats were randomly divided into four groups: control (saline), OHA/F127 hydrogel, ZR-CDs, and ZCD@OF hydrogel. Treatments were applied on days 0, 3, 6, 9 and 12. Wound sizes were recorded on days 0, 3, 7, 10, and 14.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Histological analysis and in vivo antibacterial activity evaluation
ce:section.ce:para: On days 7 and 14 post-surgery, the rats were euthanized, and tissues from the wound areas were collected. Histological assessments, using Hematoxylin-Eosin (H&E) staining and Masson's Trichrome staining, were conducted to examine tissue structure and pathological changes. Immunohistochemical (IHC) analyses were employed to assess angiogenesis and inflammation using specific antibodies including anti-CD31, anti-TNF-α, and anti-IL-6. To further evaluate the biosafety of the hydrogel, major organs were harvested on day 14 post-treatment and subjected to H&E staining for histopathological examination. To evaluate the antibacterial activity of ZCD@OF hydrogel in vivo, skin samples from the wound area were collected on day 7, which were then diluted and cultured on LB agar plates to count CFU. Antibacterial rate (%) = (CFU of control − CFU of experimental groups)/CFU of control × 100 %.

All quantitative analyses were performed using GraphPad Prism 9 software. One-way ANOVA followed by Tukey's multiple comparison test was used to evaluate differences among multiple groups. Values with P < 0.05 were considered statistically significant.

In this study, the synthesis conditions for ZR-CDs were systematically optimized. Experimental results demonstrated that a reaction temperature of 200 °C and a zinc acetate-to-rutin mass ratio of 0.5 constituted the optimal parameters for ZR-CDs synthesis, achieving the highest synthetic yield of 11.6 % (Table S1). In contrast, a lower temperature of 180 °C resulted in a significantly reduced synthetic yield (7.3 %), indicating insufficient thermal energy for effective carbonization. Conversely, elevating the temperature to 220 °C led to a synthetic yield decline (8.72 %), which is likely attributable to excessive temperatures causing the formation of amorphous carbon structures and larger particles. Systematic investigation of zinc acetate ratios revealed that the 0.5 ratio maximized product formation, whereas excessive zinc acetate (ratio 1:1) drastically suppressed yields to 3.1 %. This phenomenon may arise from aggregation or competitive coordination effects that hinder carbon dot assembly.

TEM analysis ( Fig. 1A) showed that the ZR-CDs exhibit a well-dispersed spherical structure with a limited size range. The particles demonstrate an average diameter of 7.86 ± 1.38 nm, indicative of their nanoscale size and uniformity (Fig. 1A). The elemental spatial distribution of ZR-CDs was characterized through elemental mapping analysis. As demonstrated in Fig. 1B, the characteristic elements including C, N, O, and Zn exhibit homogeneous distribution patterns across the ZR-CDs, confirming the successful incorporation of nitrogen and zinc atoms into the carbon dots. The hydrodynamic diameter of ZR-CDs was measured to be 11.02 nm through dynamic light scattering (DLS) analysis (Fig. 1C). This value is slightly larger than the particle size observed via TEM, which can be attributed to the presence of a hydration layer on the surface of the ZR-CDs, aligns with previous observations in related research [46,47]. The optical characteristics of ZR-CDs were examined through UV-Vis spectroscopy. The absorption spectrum displayed a significant band at 330 nm, typically associated with π-π* transitions within the sp² hybridized carbon atom network. Additionally, under excitation at 286 nm, the ZR-CDs exhibited an emission peak at 575 nm, indicative of their photoluminescent properties (Fig. 1D).

The surface chemical characteristics of ZR-CDs were investigated by FT-IR spectroscopy (Fig. 1E). A broad absorption band at 3406 cm⁻¹ arises from overlapping O–H (hydroxyl) and N–H (amine) stretching vibrations, confirming the presence of phenolic –OH groups (from rutin) and amine moieties (introduced by EDA). The peak at 2925 cm⁻¹ corresponds to aliphatic C–H stretching (CH₂/CH₃), while the signal at 1566 cm⁻¹ reflects aromatic CC vibrations from the preserved flavonoid backbone of rutin. The peak at 1413 cm⁻¹ is assigned to symmetric COO⁻ stretching, and the band at 1124 cm⁻¹ originates from C–O stretching in ether/alcohol groups. Notably, the distinct peak at 1069 cm⁻¹ corresponds to C–N stretching vibrations, confirming nitrogen doping via EDA-derived –NH₂ groups or C–N bond formation during carbonization. These results collectively demonstrate the successful synthesis of N doped CDs with surface functionalities inherited from rutin and EDA precursors.

XRD patterns (Fig. 1F) showed that both nitrogen-doped rutin-derived carbon dots (NR-CDs) and ZR-CDs had diffraction peaks corresponding to the (002) plane of graphite at 2θ values between 22° and 25°. Peaks at 31.8°, 45.4°, and 56.6° in the ZR-CDs pattern corresponded to the (200), (211), and (301) planes of ZnO, respectively (PDF Card No.97-067-0356). XPS analysis provided detailed insights into the elemental composition and chemical states of ZR-CDs. The survey spectrum (Fig. 1G) displayed distinct peaks corresponding to C 1s at 284.8 eV, N 1s at 399.6 eV, O 1s at 531.7 eV, and Zn 2p at 1023 eV and 1047 eV, confirming the presence of carbon, nitrogen, oxygen, and zinc. Quantitative analysis revealed that carbon constituted 68.76 % of the elemental composition, followed by oxygen (22.85 %), nitrogen (6.94 %), and zinc (1.45 %). The high-resolution C 1s spectrum (Fig. 1H) exhibited three characteristic peaks at 284.8 eV, 286.2 eV, and 288.4 eV, corresponding to sp²-hybridized graphitic carbon (CC/C-C), oxygen/nitrogen-linked bonds (C-O/C-N), and carbonyl groups (CO), respectively. This spectral profile suggests a hybrid structure combining a graphitic framework with abundant oxygen- and nitrogen-containing surface moieties. Furthermore, the N 1s spectrum (Fig. 1I) displayed two distinct peaks at 399.5 eV and 400.2 eV, which were assigned to pyridinic nitrogen and pyrrolic/amino nitrogen configurations. These features further confirm the successful incorporation of nitrogen heteroatoms through both aromatic heterocyclic structures and amine functional groups. The O 1 s spectrum further confirmed the presence of multiple oxygen-containing functional groups, with peaks at 531.5 eV for CO and 532.6 eV for C-OH or C-O-C bonds (Fig. 1J). The Zn 2p spectrum exhibited peaks at 1021 eV and 1044 eV (Fig. 1K). The zeta potential analysis of ZR-CDs revealed a surface charge of −6.53 ± 1.53 mV (Fig. 1L).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antioxidant properties of ZR-CDs and R-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: This study systematically evaluated the antioxidant activities of ZR-CDs and R-CDs through ABTS⁺•, DPPH•, and O₂•⁻ scavenging assays (Fig. S1A-C, Fig. 1M–O). Experimental results demonstrated that ZR-CDs displayed markedly superior, concentration-dependent antioxidant activity compared to R-CDs. In the ABTS radical scavenging assay, ZR-CDs displayed pronounced concentration-dependent elimination of ABTS⁺• radicals. The scavenging efficiency increased from 23.0 ± 3.3 % at 5 μg/mL to 94.7 ± 2.9 % at 30 μg/mL, significantly surpassing that of R-CDs at equivalent concentration (83.8 ± 5.0 %, p < 0.05) (Fig. 1M). The DPPH radical scavenging results further validated the antioxidant superiority of ZR-CDs. With increasing concentrations from 5 μg/mL to 30 μg/mL, the scavenging rate of ZR-CDs against DPPH radicals rose from 26.6 ± 2.2 % to 72.7 ± 3.0 %, significantly higher than R-CDs at the same top concentration (61.7 ± 4.3 %, p < 0.01) (Fig. 1N). Additionally, the superoxide anion scavenging assay revealed exceptional performance of ZR-CDs. Using a modified NBT reduction method, ZR-CDs demonstrated 52.8 ± 4.7 % O₂•⁻ scavenging efficiency at 150 μg/mL, outperforming R-CDs (47.0 ± 5.7 %). At lower concentration (50 μg/mL), ZR-CDs maintained 39.2 ± 3.1 scavenging activity, significantly exceeding that of R-CDs (27.3 ± 3.2 %, p < 0.01) (Fig. 1O).

In summary, ZR-CDs demonstrate significantly enhanced antioxidant capabilities through effective scavenging of various free radicals. Doping modulation not only optimizes the electronic structure and energy level distribution of carbon dots but also introduces novel surface functional groups and defect states, thereby improving their radical scavenging capacity. In terms of antioxidant performance, nitrogen doping facilitates the formation of electron-donating groups such as hydroxyl and amino residues, which enhance the electron transfer capability during radical interactions [48–50]. Pyrrolic N possesses a lone pair that enhances reduction of free radicals, while pyridinic N contributes to π-conjugation and radical adsorption [51,52]. These findings highlight their potential value in therapeutic applications for oxidative stress-related diseases, suggesting promising prospects in biomedical interventions against pathological conditions associated with reactive oxygen species imbalance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of ZCD@OF hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of the hydrogel begins with the oxidation of hyaluronic acid in deionized water using sodium periodate, introducing aldehyde groups required for subsequent grafting. The 4 % (w/v) oxidized hyaluronic acid is then mixed with a 20 % (w/v) solution of Pluronic F-127 and stirred for 4 h at 4 °C, followed by incubation at 4 °C overnight to form a uniform mixture. The 10 % (w/v) ZR-CDs are subsequently incorporated into the hydrogel matrix, utilizing Schiff base reactions, hydrogen bonding, and hydrophilic-hydrophobic interactions to embed these within the hydrogel matrix. The resulting hydrogel exhibited temperature-sensitive properties, making it injectable and adaptable to various shapes, which enhances its potential applications in wound healing.

SEM image ( Fig. 2B) of the ZCD@OF hydrogel reveals a well-defined porous architecture, with pores averaging around 28.86 ± 5.59 µm in size. In comparison, the pure Pluronic F-127 hydrogel exhibits larger pores, averaging 54.93 ± 9.22 µm. This difference in pore size is attributed to the additional physical crosslinking and entanglements provided by OHA, which results in a denser and more stable network. Additionally, the porous structure facilitates the absorption of wound exudates, further supporting its potential for medical applications [53].

FT-IR spectroscopy further confirmed the successful integration of hydrogel constituents (Fig. 2C). In the spectrum of OHA, an absorption band at 1721 cm⁻¹, attributable to the CO stretching vibration of aldehyde groups, verifies the efficient introduction of aldehyde functionalities into the polymer backbone. Upon formation of the ZCD@OF composite hydrogel, this aldehyde peak completely disappears, concomitant with the emergence of a new band at 1645 cm⁻¹ corresponding to the CN stretching vibration of imine (Schiff base) linkages. This shift indicates that the aldehyde groups on OHA have undergone Schiff base condensation with amine moieties on ZR-CDs, yielding dynamic imine crosslinks within the hydrogel network. Additionally, the spectrum of the composite hydrogel displays a distinct absorption at 1110 cm⁻¹, assignable to the asymmetric C–O–C stretching vibration of ether bonds inherent to Pluronic F-127. The presence of this band confirms the successful incorporation of Pluronic F-127 chains into the three-dimensional hydrogel matrix. Together, these FT-IR observations substantiate the formation of a multi-component hydrogel: aldehyde functionalization of HA, Schiff base crosslinking with ZR-CDs, and physical entanglement or integration of Pluronic F-127, thereby validating the designed composite structure for subsequent functional assays.

The ZCD@OF hydrogel exhibits significant thermosensitivity, with its reversible sol-gel transition property visually demonstrated by the vial inversion method: it remains liquid at 4 °C, facilitating injection, and rapidly transitions to a gel state at 37 °C (physiological temperature), stably staying at the bottom of the vial (Fig. 2A). Injection tests at room temperature show that the ZCD@OF hydrogel demonstrates excellent injectability, smoothly extruding through the syringe to form clear patterns without any resistance (Fig. 2E). These attributes underscore the superior adaptability of the material to various wound morphologies, which is beneficial in preventing suboptimal therapeutic outcomes due to incongruities between the hydrogel and the wound area. Furthermore, this reversible thermosensitive sol-gel transition property improves its practicality in clinical applications.

Rheological analysis further confirms the hydrogel's thermosensitivity and dynamic mechanical properties of injectability. Temperature sweep test results (Fig. 2D) indicate that both the storage modulus (G′) and loss modulus (G″) exhibit a significant increase as the temperature rises from 4 °C to 40 °C. Particularly at around 28°C, a significant increase in G′ indicates a noticeable phase transition of the hydrogel at its lower critical solution temperature (LCST), which is close to the superficial temperature of human extremities [54]. This implies that upon contact with human skin, the hydrogel's mechanical strength significantly increases, providing more stable and lasting support [55]. Additionally, the hydrogel also exhibits shear-thinning behavior, where at 25°C (simulating room temperature conditions), the viscosity of the hydrogel substantially decreases with an increase in shear rate (Fig. 2E), demonstrating excellent injectability and suitability for clinical applications.

In summary, the ZCD@OF hydrogel demonstrates pronounced temperature responsiveness, maintaining a liquid state at room temperature for easy application and transitioning to a gel at body temperature to ensure stable functioning. These properties, combined with the hydrogel's injectability and adaptability, make it ideal option for managing complex skin infections.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro evaluation of swelling, degradation and drug release properties of ZCD@OF hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, we systematically evaluated the swelling behavior of lyophilized ZCD@OF hydrogels in PBS (pH 7.4) at 37 °C. As shown in Fig. 2F, the hydrogels exhibited a rapid initial swelling phase, followed by a plateau reached within 4 h. At equilibrium, the ZCD@OF hydrogel achieved a maximum swelling ratio of 860.5 ± 35.9 %

The degradation rate and release characteristics of the ZCD@OF hydrogel were assessed to evaluate its potential for sustained drug release applications. The biodegradability of the hydrogel was evaluated using a PBS solution containing 2 U/mL hyaluronidase at 37 °C. As illustrated in Fig. 2G, the hydrogel demonstrated near complete dissolution within 72 h, indicating excellent biodegradability and stability. These findings suggest that the ZCD@OF hydrogel is suitable for further in vivo studies.

In addition, the release profile of ZR-CDs from the ZCD@OF hydrogel was investigated. As depicted in Fig. 2H, the release of ZR-CDs was initially rapid within the first 8 h (cumulative release: 63.3 ± 5.1 %), followed by a slower, sustained release until completion at 72 h. This release pattern confirms that ZR-CDs were effectively encapsulated within the ZCD@OF hydrogel matrix and exhibited sustained release properties.

The antibacterial efficacy of the ZCD@OF hydrogel was assessed against S. aureus using the colony-forming unit (CFU) method ( Fig. 3A). Statistical analysis revealed that ZCD@OF hydrogel exhibited significantly enhanced antimicrobial efficacy (94.7 ± 3.5 %) compared to the OHA/F127 hydrogel group (12.9 ± 4.1 %) and ZR-CDs group (77.2 ± 4.6 %) (Fig. 3B). These findings suggest that the ZCD@OF hydrogel possesses enhanced antibacterial activity. To further evaluate the antibacterial ability of the ZCD@OF hydrogel, SYTO 9/PI live/dead staining was employed. The control group displayed predominantly green fluorescence, signifying viable bacteria. Conversely, the ZR-CDs group showed increased red fluorescence, indicating bacterial membrane disruption and cell death. Notably, the ZCD@OF hydrogel group exhibited strongest red fluorescence with minimal green fluorescence, suggesting effective bacterial membrane destruction (Fig. 3C). Quantitative analysis of bacterial mortality confirmed that ZCD@OF induced 76.3 ± 7.3 % bacterial death, significantly surpassing other experimental groups (Fig. 3D), thereby substantiating its potent bactericidal effects against S. aureus.

Crystal violet staining revealed that both ZCD@OF extract and ZR-CDs substantially inhibited S. aureus biofilm formation. Specifically, treatment with the ZCD@OF extract achieved a biofilm inhibition rate of 75.1 ± 2.3 %, while ZR-CDs treatment reached 70.2 ± 5.0 %. In contrast, the OHA/F127 hydrogel group exhibited only an 11.4 ± 2.8 % biofilm inhibition rate. Statistical analysis confirmed that both ZCD@OF and ZR-CDs treatments were significantly more effective than the OHA/F127 hydrogel (P < 0.0001).

ZR-CDs exhibit a synergistic mechanism of action against both planktonic bacteria and mature biofilms. For planktonic Gram-positive bacteria such as S. aureus, the nanoscale ZR-CDs readily penetrate the thick peptidoglycan layer of Gram-positive bacteria and insert into the cytoplasmic membrane, inducing structural perturbations and compromising barrier functionality. The introduced amino groups on the nitrogen-doped carbon dot surface become protonated under physiological conditions and engage in electrostatic attractions with anionic components of the bacterial cell envelope [56]. This interaction induces local membrane potential dissipation and enhances membrane permeability, facilitating further carbon-dot penetration and exacerbating membrane damage. The increased permeability exacerbates the membrane damage, as confirmed by PI staining, which shows a marked increase in fluorescence intensity, indicating significant membrane compromise [57]. The nanoscale and surface charge modulation of ZR-CDs enable effective penetration into mature biofilm architectures, exerting antimicrobial effects within the biofilm matrix. Furthermore, the carbonization of rutin during synthesis preserves its multi-hydroxylated aromatic backbone, which retains intrinsic antimicrobial and antioxidant activities [58,59]. Additionally, the Zn²⁺/ZnO component in the system provides bactericidal synergistic effects. Previous studies have shown that Zinc ions can bind to and destabilize membrane phospholipids, leading to increased permeability and cell lysis. Moreover, Zn²⁺ can inactivate crucial bacterial enzymes by displacing native metal cofactors or by direct coordination to thiol and carboxylate groups, thereby disrupting metabolic pathways [60–62]. Additionally, the ZR-CDs effectively prevent the early stages of biofilm formation by inhibiting initial bacterial colonization. The system suppresses the early-phase seeding of biofilms, effectively preventing the establishment and growth of mature biofilm structures.

Comprehensive biocompatibility assessments were conducted on ZR-CDs and the ZCD@OF hydrogel. The cytotoxicity of ZR-CDs was investigated using the CCK-8 assay on L929 cells, HUVECs, and Raw 264.7 cells. After 24 h of exposure to varying concentrations of ZR-CDs, cell viability remained above 80 % across all cell lines, indicating low cytotoxicity ( Fig. 4A, B, C). Fluorescence microscopy with live/dead staining further validated these findings, showing that the majority of HUVECs and L929 cells not only survived but also maintained normal morphology, evidenced by robust green fluorescence and minimal signs of cell death (Fig. 4E, S2A). Quantitative analysis of the live/dead cell ratio (Fig. 4F, S2C) confirmed that over 80 % of the cells remained viable, reinforcing the minimal cytotoxicity of the ZR-CDs. Additionally, hemolysis testing showed that the hemolysis rate of ZR-CDs remained below 3 % across all concentrations tested, further supporting their excellent hemocompatibility (Fig. 4D).

The cytotoxicity of the ZCD@OF hydrogel was similarly assessed using the CCK-8 assay. L929 cells were exposed to hydrogel extracts for 24 h, and cell viability reached 85.2 ± 4.8 %, indicating that the hydrogel matrix also demonstrated favorable biocompatibility (Fig. S2E). Live/dead staining analysis showed that most cells exhibited healthy, spindle-shaped morphology with bright green fluorescence, and only a small proportion of dead cells were observed (Fig. S2B). Quantitative analysis of the live/dead ratio (Fig. S2D) showed that the live cell ratio reached 84.3 ± 1.8 %, corroborating these findings. Hemolysis testing further confirmed the hydrogel's excellent hemocompatibility, with a hemolysis rate below 3 % at all concentrations tested (Fig. S2F).
In summary, both the ZR-CDs and the ZCD@OF hydrogel demonstrated excellent biocompatibility, suggesting their potential for safe use in biomedical applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cellular ROS scavenging effects of ZR-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Infectious wounds are characterized by increased ROS levels and prolonged oxidative stress [63]. To evaluate the protective capabilities of ZR-CDs against cellular oxidative stress, L929 cells and HUVECs were subjected to H2O2 induced oxidative damage. The cells were incubated with varying levels of ZR-CDs, utilizing DCFH-DA as an indicator of ROS. Following H2O2 treatment, there was a significant increase in ROS levels, as indicated by enhanced green fluorescence intensity. The addition of ZR-CDs resulted in a dose-dependent reduction in intracellular ROS signals of L929 cells and HUVECs, as shown in Fig. 5A, C. Quantitative analysis, as depicted in Fig. 5B, D, revealed that ZR-CDs significantly reduced ROS levels in both L929 cells and HUVECs. These findings underscore the potent antioxidant effects of ZR-CDs in mitigating ROS levels.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Impact of ZR-CDs on cell migration and angiogenesis under oxidative stress
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the impact of ZR-CDs on HUVECs and L929 cells under oxidative stress, we performed a series of assays to assess cell migration and angiogenesis. Initially, wound healing assays were conducted to measure cell migration capabilities. Our results indicate that the wound closure rates at 0 and 24 h for cells treated with various concentrations of ZR-CDs were significantly improved compared to the cells treated with H2O2 (Fig. 5E, S3A), suggesting enhanced cell motility under stress conditions. Further analysis using Transwell migration assays supported these findings, showing a substantial rise in the quantity of migrating cells in the ZR-CDs treated groups after 24 h compared to controls (Fig. 5G, S3C). This reinforces the notion that ZR-CDs effectively promote cell migration in an environment of oxidative stress.

Additionally, we utilized Matrigel tube formation assays to evaluate the effects of ZR-CDs on angiogenesis (Fig. 5I). HUVECs treated with ZR-CDs demonstrated a notable increase in node numbers and capillary lengths formed after 6 h, compared to cells treated with H2O2 alone (Fig. 5J, K). This suggests that ZR-CDs not only support cell migration but also enhance angiogenic processes under oxidative stress conditions.

Overall, our findings highlight the potential of ZR-CDs to preserve cellular functions under oxidative stress, which could be beneficial for therapeutic applications in tissue regeneration and repair.

The therapeutic potential of the ZCD@OF hydrogel was evaluated in a rat model using infected full-thickness skin defects. This study builds on our previous findings regarding the hydrogel's in vitro antibacterial, antioxidant, and anti-inflammatory properties. Wounds were randomly assigned to four treatment groups: Control (saline), OHA/F127, ZR-CDs, and ZCD@OF, according to the surgical and treatment protocols illustrated in Fig. 6A. Analysis of photographs taken on days 0, 3, 7, 10, and 14 (Fig. 6B, C) demonstrated that the ZCD@OF hydrogel significantly enhanced the regeneration of skin tissue and the closure of wounds. Statistical analysis confirmed that the ZCD@OF group outperformed the control group at all time points (Fig. 6D).

To assess the in vivo antimicrobial efficacy of the ZCD@OF, sterile swabs were used to collect samples from around the wounds on day 7 for bacterial analysis. Bacterial counts were determined by plating the samples on LB agar (Fig. 6E). Quantitative results showed that the antibacterial rate of the ZCD@OF group was 87.1 ± 5.0 %, which was significantly higher compared to other treatment groups, supporting the antibacterial effects observed in vitro (Fig. 6F).

Histological examination at 14 days post-treatment, using H&E and Masson stains, revealed that wounds treated with ZCD@OF displayed the best healing outcomes, followed by the ZR-CDs group, the control and OHA/F127 groups exhibited significantly poorer wound healing. Enlarged images from H&E and Masson’s staining showed that the wounds in the ZCD@OF group exhibited a more complete epidermal layer and denser, more organized collagen fibers in the dermis compared to the other groups ( Fig. 7A, B). Quantitative analyses indicated that the ZCD@OF group had a significantly higher collagen fiber coverage rate of 71.6 ± 5.1 % compared to the control group (Fig. 7D). Additionally, the organization of collagen fibers appeared more structured in the ZCD@OF group, suggesting mature granulation tissue formation rather than simple scar formation. Vascular remodeling, which is key in tissue repair, was evaluated by the hydrogel's effect on angiogenesis using CD31 IHC staining (Fig. 7C). The quantitative results showed notably elevated expression of CD31 in the ZCD@OF group (Fig. 7E), indicative of increased vascular density and the development of more mature vessel structures.

The expression of inflammatory markers IL-6 and TNF-α was assessed on day 7 post-treatment to evaluate the anti-inflammatory properties of the hydrogel, utilizing IHC staining (Fig. 7C). Quantitative analyses revealed that the ZCD@OF group exhibited significantly lower levels of IL-6 and TNF-α compared to the control group (Fig. 7F, G), underscoring the hydrogel’s potent anti-inflammatory effects.

In summary, the ZCD@OF hydrogel not only facilitated enhanced wound closure and tissue regeneration but also exhibited significant antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory effects, supporting its potential utility in clinical applications for wound healing. Furthermore, to evaluate the biosafety of the hydrogel, H&E staining was applied to the visceral sections of the treated animals, which showed no significant damage in any treatment group, affirming the biocompatibility of the hydrogel (Figs. S2G).

In this study, we successfully synthesized ZR-CDs via a green one-step hydrothermal method and integrated them into a temperature-sensitive hydrogel composed of oxidized hyaluronic acid and Pluronic F127. Our findings demonstrate that ZR-CDs possess strong ROS scavenging abilities, contributing to their effectiveness in mitigating oxidative stress and preserving critical cellular functions such as migration and angiogenesis. The composite hydrogel, ZCD@OF showed significant antibacterial activity in both in vitro and in vivo studies, particularly against S. aureus. In a rat model with infected full-thickness skin wounds, the hydrogel not only facilitated rapid wound closure but also supported collagen deposition and promoted angiogenesis, while concurrently suppressing inflammatory markers. Overall, these findings highlight the ZCD@OF hydrogel's potential therapeutic effectiveness as a multifunctional therapeutic agent and underscore the significant potential of integrating advanced materials and nanotechnology to develop sustainable, effective solutions for biomedical applications.
ROS: Reactive oxygen species
CDs: Carbon dots
ZR-CDs: Nitrogen and zinc-doped rutin-derived carbon dots
NR-CDs: Nitrogen-doped rutin-derived carbon dots
R-CDs: Ruin-derived carbon dots
F127: Pluronic F-127
OHA: Oxidized hyaluronic acid
DMEM: Dulbecco's modified eagle medium
FBS: Fetal bovine serum
HUVECs: Human umbilical vein endothelial cells
TEM: Transmission electron microscope
UV-Vis: Ultraviolet-Visible
PL: Photoluminescence
FT-IR: Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy
XPS: X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy
XRD: X-ray Diffraction
SEM: Scanning electron microscope
ABTS: 2′-azino-bis(3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid)
DPPH: 1-diphenyl-2-picrylhydrazyl
NBT: Nitroblue tetrazolium
LB: Luria-Bertani
CFU: Colony-forming units
CLSM: Confocal laser scanning microscope
CCK-8: Cell Counting Kit-8
PI: Propidium iodide
DCFH-DA: 2′,7′-dichlorodihydrofluorescein diacetate
H&E: Hematoxylin-Eosin
IHC: Immunohistochemistry
LCST: Lower critical solution temperature
